message.content: Adversaries may exploit the search order used by Windows to load programs, executing their malicious payloads by placing them in directories where the calling program resides. This technique, known as Path Interception by Search Order Hijacking, leverages the fact that some programs do not specify full paths when calling other executables. By placing a malicious file with the same name as the intended executable in the initiating program's directory, adversaries can ensure their payload is executed instead. This method differs from DLL Search Order Hijacking but similarly exploits the search order for executable files. Tools like Empire and PowerSploit can discover and exploit these vulnerabilities. The impact includes unauthorized execution of malicious code, potentially leading to system compromise. Mitigation strategies involve ensuring programs specify full paths when calling executables, monitoring and restricting write permissions to directories where executables are located, and employing application whitelisting to prevent unauthorized programs from running.
